result:
If I could change the world in one way, I would dissolve the illusion of separation—the deeply ingrained belief that we are isolated selves, fundamentally disconnected from each other, from other living beings, and from the fabric of existence itself. So much of our cruelty, our fear, our loneliness, and our reckless destruction springs from this single, persistent error: the conviction that your well-being and mine are somehow separate equations, that what happens to the oceans or the forests or the stranger across the city has nothing to do with the silent pulse of our own lives.

I don’t mean a superficial “we are all one” slogan or a fleeting sentiment of unity. I mean a direct, undeniable, permanent shift in perception—as visceral as feeling your own heartbeat—where every act of harm is felt immediately as a wound upon oneself, and every gesture of kindness is known as self-care. A world where empathy isn’t a virtue you strain to practice but the native language of experience. From that single change, all other transformations would unspool: the end of exploitation, the careful tending of the natural world, the dissolution of tribalism and the hierarchies that devalue entire categories of being.

I’d change the lens, not the picture—and trust that what we saw through it would lead us home.
